questions.question: Why does a cache need an eviction policy at all?
options: To encrypt the cached data
Because memory is finite, so when the cache is full a new entry forces an old one out — and which one matters
To make the cache slower on purpose
Eviction policies are only for databases
questions.explanation: A cache holds only a small fraction of the data. Once full, every new entry requires removing an existing one. Evicting a hot item means an immediate miss and reload (self-inflicted), so the policy choice directly determines the hit ratio.

questions.question: What does LRU (Least Recently Used) bet on?
options: That the oldest-inserted data is the least useful
Temporal locality — data used recently is likely to be used again soon
That all data is equally likely to be accessed
That frequency never changes
questions.explanation: LRU evicts whatever hasn't been touched for the longest time, betting on temporal locality. Most real workloads exhibit this (recently used → reused soon), which is why LRU and its approximations are the common default.

questions.question: How does an LRU cache achieve O(1) for both lookup and marking most-recently-used?
options: By scanning a sorted array on each access
A hash map gives O(1) key lookup; a doubly linked list gives O(1) move-to-front and tail removal
By using a single linked list only
By re-sorting all entries after every access
questions.explanation: The hash map maps each key to its node for O(1) lookup. The doubly linked list orders nodes by recency (MRU at head, LRU at tail); because each node has prev/next pointers, you can unlink and move it to the head, or remove the tail, all in O(1).

questions.question: On the access sequence A B C A B D with an LRU cache of size 3, what gets evicted when D is accessed?
options: A, because it was inserted first
C, because it is the least recently used at that point
B, because it was accessed twice
Nothing is evicted
questions.explanation: After A B C A B, the recency order (most to least) is B, A, C. Adding D requires evicting the least recently used, which is C. LRU correctly keeps A and B (recently reused) and sheds the genuinely cold C. FIFO would have wrongly evicted A.

questions.question: What is LRU's classic failure case?
options: It cannot store more than 100 entries
A large one-time scan (many unique keys read once) evicts the entire hot set — cache pollution
It is too slow to be practical
It only works with integer keys
questions.explanation: If a scan reads more distinct keys than the cache holds, each new key evicts a hot entry to store data that's never read again, flushing the useful hot set — LRU pollution. LFU resists this (one-time accesses never build frequency), and real systems use hybrids like TinyLFU/ARC.
